race.name: Marmotte Granfondo Sestriere
race.tagline: The only granfondo in the world with the Colle delle Finestre. 7.8 km of legendary Giro d'Italia dirt at 2,178 meters. Summit finish at Sestriere.

terrain_types: Legendary unpaved summit road (Colle delle Finestre)
vitals.registration: Online via granfondosestriere.it or ENDU platform. Early-bird and package pricing available. Strade Bianche della Via Lattea package (2 races) ~EUR90. Individual entry estimated EUR50-70.
vitals.prize_purse: None (age-group rankings and commemorative jersey for first 1,500 registrants)
vitals.aid_stations: Feed stations at key points along the route. Water, energy bars, fruit, and Italian fare at summit zones.
vitals.cutoff_time: Intermediate cutoffs enforced. Riders must maintain required pace through major climbs.
distance_options: Granfondo: 103.6 km (64 mi), 3,170 m gain — full route with Colle delle Finestre
Mediofondo: 79 km (49 mi), 2,370 m gain — shorter route, still includes Colle delle Finestre
2026 routes: 131 km and 79 km options announced

climate.description: Early July in the Piedmont Alps delivers warm valley starts in Cesana Torinese (18-25C at 1,354m elevation) that give way to cooler summit conditions at Colle delle Finestre (2,178m) where temperatures can drop to 8-14C with wind. The exposed gravel summit road amplifies the chill. Afternoon sun in the Susa Valley between climbs can reach 28-30C. Less extreme temperature swings than the French Alpine Galibier events, but the altitude and exposed dirt road create their own conditions.
challenges: Colle delle Finestre summit (2,178m): 8-14C with wind on exposed gravel road
Valley sections: 25-30C in July afternoon sun
Afternoon thunderstorms possible over Piedmont peaks — build rapidly
Exposed final 7.8 km of dirt road offers no shelter from sun, wind, or rain

terrain.primary: Paved Alpine roads with iconic unpaved summit
terrain.surface: Well-maintained Italian provincial roads through Susa and Chisone Valleys. All paved except the legendary final 7.8 km of Colle delle Finestre, which transitions from tarmac to hard-packed dirt and gravel with embedded stones. The gravel section is rideable on road bikes with 25-28mm tires but demands attention on gradients up to 14%.
features: Colle delle Finestre (2,178m) — 18.5 km at 9.2% average, final 7.8 km on unpaved dirt road with gradients to 14%
Colle del Sestriere (2,035m) — the Olympic ski station finish, a steady alpine grinder
Susa Valley descent — fast sweeping road from Cesana through Oulx to the valley floor
Val Chisone — the return climb through Pragelato back to the Via Lattea ski domain

gravel_god_rating.tier_note: Italian sister event to La Marmotte Alpes, formerly branded Marmotte Granfondo Sestriere. Now independent as Granfondo Sestriere — Colle delle Finestre. The only granfondo featuring the legendary unpaved Finestre summit. T2 by score (69).
gravel_god_rating.score_note: technicality=2 for the 7.8 km unpaved Finestre summit (not pure road, not pure gravel). elevation=4 for 3,170m across two major alpine ascents. experience=4 for the iconic tarmac-to-dirt transition that defined Giro d'Italia stages. cultural_impact=3: the Finestre itself is legendary, but the event is only 10 editions old.

logistics.explanation: Competent Italian granfondo organization with chip timing, feed stations, and medical support. The 2025 move to start from Cesana Torinese improved safety on the descent into the Susa Valley. Turin airport (TRN) is 80 km away — roughly 1.5 hours by car or bus, making access easier than many deep-Alpine events. Sestriere and Cesana Torinese are small ski resort towns with limited but adequate summer infrastructure. Packet pickup and event logistics are straightforward for a field of 1,000-1,500 riders. Not the polished machine of the French Marmotte, but functional and improving with each edition.

length.explanation: 103.6 km (64 miles) for the Granfondo route is moderate by European granfondo standards — shorter than the 177 km French Marmotte or the 138 km Maratona dles Dolomites. But the 3,170m of climbing packed into that distance means the climbing density is high: over 30 meters of elevation per kilometer. The 79 km Mediofondo option still includes Colle delle Finestre, making it an excellent choice for riders who want the signature climb without the full-day commitment. The 2026 edition extends the long route to 131 km, signaling that organizers want to push the event toward more serious territory.

technicality.explanation: This is a road granfondo with one massive asterisk: the 7.8 km of unpaved road on Colle delle Finestre. After 10 km of steady tarmac climbing, the road abruptly transitions to hard-packed dirt and embedded gravel — the same surface that has defined Giro d'Italia stages since 2005. The gravel is rideable on road bikes (25mm+ tires recommended) but demands attention: loose stones on gradients up to 14%, exposed ruts, and the psychological shock of leaving tarmac at 1,700m altitude with 500m of climbing still to go. The rest of the route is standard well-maintained Italian provincial road. It is not a gravel event, but it is not purely road either.

elevation.explanation: 3,170 meters of climbing on the Granfondo route is serious — equivalent to climbing from sea level to the summit of a major Alpine pass, then doing it again. The Colle delle Finestre alone accounts for 1,694m of that total, with a relentless 9.2% average gradient over 18.5 km. Add the return climb to Sestriere (2,035m) through Val Chisone, and you have a route where you are almost always either climbing or descending. It does not reach the 5,000m+ territory of the full Marmotte Alpes, but for a 100 km event, this is emphatically vertical.

climate.explanation: Early July in the Piedmont Alps delivers manageable but variable conditions. Valley starts around Cesana Torinese (1,354m) are comfortably warm at 18-22C, but the Finestre summit at 2,178m will be 8-14C with wind — and on the exposed gravel road, there is no shelter from anything. The temperature range is less extreme than the French Galibier events (no sub-zero wind chill), but afternoon thunderstorms are a real possibility and can arrive fast in the Susa Valley. Bring arm warmers for the Finestre descent; you will regret not having them.

altitude.explanation: The Colle delle Finestre tops out at 2,178m and the finish at Sestriere sits at 2,035m — meaningful altitude that will affect riders from sea level, but not the oxygen-debt territory of 2,600m+ passes like the Galibier or Stelvio. The start in Cesana Torinese at 1,354m means you never drop below moderate altitude during the entire event. Acclimatization helps but is not strictly necessary for most trained riders. The altitude effect is noticeable on the steeper Finestre gradients (9-14%) where every watt counts.

adventure.explanation: This is where the Granfondo Sestriere separates itself from every other road granfondo in the world. The transition from tarmac to dirt on Colle delle Finestre is one of cycling's most iconic moments — the same surface where Savoldelli lost the Giro, where Froome launched his legendary 80 km attack, where Landa and Aru wrote their names into cycling history. Riding that gravel on a road bike at 2,000m, surrounded by larch forests and Alpine peaks, with the knowledge that the professionals suffered on this exact road, creates an adventure that no fully paved event can match. The Susa Valley, 2006 Winter Olympics infrastructure, and the Via Lattea ski domain add layers of scenic and cultural depth.

prestige.explanation: The Colle delle Finestre has enormous prestige in cycling — it is arguably the most famous single climb in modern Giro d'Italia history. But the Granfondo Sestriere event itself is young. Founded in 2015 (initially as Marmotte Granfondo Sestriere), it separated from the Marmotte brand and reached its 10th edition in 2025. It does not carry the institutional weight of the 40-year-old French Marmotte, the Maratona dles Dolomites, or even the Sportful Dolomiti Race. The prestige is borrowed from the mountain rather than earned by the event — but what a mountain to borrow from.

race_quality.explanation: Well-organized for a mid-size Italian granfondo. Chip timing, multiple feed stations, medical support, and road closures are all in place. The partnership with Gobik (commemorative jerseys for early registrants) and the Specialized Granfondo Series connection show growing ambitions. Age-group rankings provide competitive structure. But this is still a regional event finding its identity after the Marmotte brand split. The field of 1,000-1,500 riders is well-managed but lacks the institutional polish of 7,000-rider events.

experience.explanation: The defining experience is the moment the tarmac ends on Colle delle Finestre. After 10 km of steady climbing on paved road, you round a corner and the asphalt simply stops. Ahead: 7.8 km of dirt road switchbacking up to 2,178m with gradients that hit 14%. This is the exact spot where Giro d'Italia history was made — where Froome attacked at 80 km from the finish, where champions were crowned and broken. Cresting the summit on that gravel, looking back down the dirt switchbacks you just climbed, and then descending to the finish at the Olympic station of Sestriere is a singular cycling experience. No other granfondo in the world offers this.

community.explanation: Predominantly Italian field with a growing international contingent. The Via Lattea ski resort community around Sestriere and Cesana Torinese provides a welcoming if seasonal backdrop — these towns understand hosting athletic events from their winter sports heritage (2006 Turin Winter Olympics). The weekend format — with a Saturday gravel race and MTB event alongside the Sunday granfondo — creates a small festival atmosphere. But the community is still developing. The field is not large enough to generate the carnival atmosphere of the Maratona or the international pilgrimage feel of the French Marmotte.

field_depth.explanation: With approximately 1,000-1,500 riders, the Granfondo Sestriere draws primarily from the Italian amateur cycling community — particularly Piedmont and Lombardy. The competitive front is thin compared to major Italian granfondos. There are no former professionals padding the start list. The depth improved when the event hit its 10th edition in 2025, but this is still a climb to Finestre, not a race to win a trophy. The field doubled from ~500 to 1,100 between 2015 and 2016, and has grown modestly since.

value.explanation: EUR50-90 for a mountain granfondo that includes cycling's most famous unpaved climb is outstanding value. The Strade Bianche della Via Lattea package at EUR90 for two events (Saturday gravel/MTB plus Sunday granfondo) is remarkable. Compare to EUR110-130 for the French Marmotte or EUR150+ for the Maratona dles Dolomites. The entry fee buys you chip timing, feed stations, medical support, road closures, and the Finestre experience. For what you get, this is one of the best-value mountain granfondos in Europe.

expenses.explanation: The Piedmont Alps are substantially cheaper than the French Alps or the Dolomites. Turin (TRN) is served by budget airlines and is only 80 km away — no 3-hour Alpine transfers required. Accommodation in Sestriere and Cesana Torinese is ski-resort priced but in the off-season (July), rates are reasonable: EUR80-140 per night for a decent hotel, with Airbnb options lower. Italian food culture means excellent pre-race carb loading without resort markup. Budget EUR600-1,200 for a US-based rider including flights, 2-3 nights accommodation, entry fee, and meals.

biased_opinion.summary: The Granfondo Sestriere exists for one reason: Colle delle Finestre. And that one reason is enough. This is the only organized cycling event in the world that sends you up the 7.8 km of unpaved road that has produced some of the most dramatic Giro d'Italia stages in modern history. The event itself is young and still finding its identity after splitting from the Marmotte brand, but the mountain does not care about branding. When the tarmac ends and the gravel begins at 1,700m, you are riding the exact surface that broke champions and made legends. Everything else — the Susa Valley, the Olympic infrastructure, the finish at Sestriere — is context for that singular experience.
strengths: The only granfondo in the world featuring the full Colle delle Finestre with its 7.8 km unpaved summit
Giro d'Italia heritage: ride the exact dirt road where Froome, Landa, Di Luca, and Aru wrote cycling history
Excellent value: EUR50-90 for a premium Alpine climbing experience
Easy access from Turin (80 km) — no deep-Alpine transfer logistics
weaknesses: Young event (10th edition in 2025) without the institutional prestige of established Italian granfondos
Small field (~1,000-1,500 riders) limits competitive depth and atmosphere
Shorter course (103 km) may underwhelm riders seeking an all-day epic
Post-Marmotte branding identity is still being established
biased_opinion.bottom_line: Come for the Finestre. That 7.8 km of dirt road at 2,000 meters, climbing at 10-14% on the same gravel that defined modern Giro stages, is worth the trip alone. Everything else is a bonus.

final_verdict.one_liner: The only granfondo in the world with Colle delle Finestre's legendary unpaved summit. Giro d'Italia dirt at 2,178 meters.
final_verdict.should_you_race: Yes if you want to ride cycling's most famous unpaved climb in an organized event. Yes if you value Giro d'Italia heritage and want to feel what the professionals felt on that dirt. Yes if you want a high-value Italian Alpine experience without the logistics of deep-Alpine events. Skip if you need a large, prestigious international granfondo. Skip if you want a 150+ km all-day epic — at 103 km, this is more focused than sprawling.
final_verdict.alternatives: If you want a larger Italian Alpine granfondo with Giro heritage: try Sportful Dolomiti Race or Maratona dles Dolomites. If you want the full Marmotte experience in the French Alps: try La Marmotte. If you want Italian gravel with more technical terrain: try Strade Bianche Gran Fondo.

logistics.airport: Turin (TRN) — 80 km, ~1.5 hours by car. Budget airline options available. Milan Malpensa (MXP) — 220 km, ~2.5 hours. Cuneo (CUF) — 80 km, limited flights.
logistics.lodging_strategy: Stay in Sestriere (finish town, 2,035m) or Cesana Torinese (start town, 1,354m) — both are ski resort towns with summer off-season rates. Hotels EUR80-140/night in July. Airbnb and apartment rentals available. Oulx (valley floor, 1,070m) offers additional options with easy access to the start. Book early for event weekend as rooms are limited in these small mountain towns.
logistics.food: Piedmontese cuisine is among Italy's finest — risotto, agnolotti pasta, brasato (braised beef), local cheeses. Carb loading is effortless. Pre-race: seek out a proper trattoria in Cesana or Sestriere. Feed stations provide Italian cycling staples: panini, fruit, energy bars, water, Coca-Cola.
logistics.packet_pickup: Event village area in Cesana Torinese or Sestriere (varies by year), day before or morning of. Manageable queues for a field of 1,000-1,500.
logistics.parking: Available in Cesana Torinese and Sestriere. Ski resort infrastructure means parking is generally adequate in summer.

history.founder: Local Piedmont cycling organization, initially in partnership with the Marmotte Granfondo series
history.origin_story: Launched in 2015 as the Italian arm of the Marmotte Granfondo Series — branded 'La Marmotte — Sestriere — Colle delle Finestre' — alongside the original French Alps Marmotte and a Pyrenees edition. The event was conceived to bring organized amateur cycling to the legendary Colle delle Finestre, whose 7.8 km of unpaved road had become one of cycling's most iconic surfaces after its 2005 Giro d'Italia debut. The inaugural edition drew approximately 500 riders. By 2016, the second edition doubled participation to 1,109 entries. The event later separated from the Marmotte brand and now operates independently as the Granfondo Sestriere — Colle delle Finestre, reaching its 10th edition in 2025 with the start relocated to Cesana Torinese. The 2006 Turin Winter Olympics legacy infrastructure in the Via Lattea ski domain provides the event's physical framework.
notable_moments: 2015: Inaugural edition as Marmotte Granfondo Sestriere, ~500 riders
2016: Second edition doubles participation to 1,109 entries; international riders appear
Event separates from Marmotte brand, rebrands as Granfondo Sestriere — Colle delle Finestre
2025: 10th anniversary edition, start relocated to Cesana Torinese for improved safety
2026: Route expanded to 131 km long option, signaling growth ambitions
history.reputation: A young event riding on the shoulders of a legendary mountain. Colle delle Finestre needs no introduction in Italian cycling — the Giro d'Italia stages of 2005, 2015, and 2018 saw to that. The Granfondo is the only organized way to ride the full climb including the unpaved summit, and that singular distinction gives it outsized significance for its age.

course_description.character: A focused Alpine assault centered on one defining climb. The route through the Susa and Chisone Valleys is scenic and demanding, but everyone is here for the same reason: Colle delle Finestre. The course builds through steady valley climbs and fast descents before delivering you to the base of the Finestre's 18.5 km ascent. The first 10 km on tarmac are relentless (9.2% average), but the real event begins when the road surface changes. The final 7.8 km on dirt — switchbacking through larch forests with gradients reaching 14% — is the granfondo's entire reason for existence. The descent and return climb to Sestriere at 2,035m ensures the legs never fully recover.

suffering_zones.label: Susa Valley Descent and Approach
suffering_zones.desc: From Cesana Torinese (1,354m), the route descends into the Susa Valley through Oulx and Salbertrand before the road turns upward toward the Finestre. The descent is fast and the valley roads are gentle, lulling riders into a false sense of security. Use this section to conserve energy — everything ahead is climbing.
suffering_zones.terrain_detail: Well-paved Italian provincial roads, gentle valley gradients

suffering_zones.label: Colle delle Finestre — Paved Section
suffering_zones.desc: The first 10.7 km of the Finestre climb are on tarmac but the gradient is unforgiving — 9.2% average with sustained sections above 10%. The road narrows as it climbs through forests, with hairpin after hairpin revealing the scale of the mountain. The knowledge that the asphalt will end adds psychological weight to every pedal stroke.
suffering_zones.terrain_detail: 18.5 km total climb, first 10.7 km paved at 9.2% average

suffering_zones.label: Colle delle Finestre — The Dirt
suffering_zones.desc: The tarmac ends. Ahead: 7.8 km of hard-packed dirt and embedded gravel climbing to 2,178m with gradients reaching 14%. This is the exact surface where Giro d'Italia stages were won and lost — where Froome attacked at 80 km from the finish, where Aru dropped Contador, where Di Luca isolated Savoldelli. The gravel is rideable on road bikes but demands full attention: loose stones on steep gradients, exposed switchbacks with no shelter, and the thin air above 2,000m compounding every effort. The summit cairn is the psychological peak of the event.
suffering_zones.terrain_detail: 7.8 km unpaved, 10-14% gradients, hard-packed dirt with embedded stones to 2,178m

suffering_zones.label: Return to Sestriere
suffering_zones.desc: After the Finestre descent through Balboutet and Usseaux, the route climbs back through Pragelato and the Via Lattea ski domain to the finish at Sestriere (2,035m). Legs that were emptied on the Finestre must now find another 800-1,000m of climbing on tired muscles. The final ramps into Sestriere are the event's quiet cruelty — not famous, not iconic, just relentlessly uphill to an Olympic finish.
suffering_zones.terrain_detail: Paved roads, climbing from ~1,000m to 2,035m via Val Chisone

course_description.signature_challenge: The 7.8 km of unpaved road on Colle delle Finestre. The transition from tarmac to dirt at approximately 1,700m altitude — on gradients that reach 14%, at oxygen levels measurably reduced by altitude — is the single most distinctive feature of any road granfondo in the world. You can train for the climbing. You cannot fully prepare for the psychological impact of losing your road surface when the gradient kicks hardest.

guide_variables.race_terrain: Paved Alpine roads with 7.8 km unpaved summit on Colle delle Finestre
guide_variables.race_weather: Warm valleys (20-28C) to cool summit conditions (8-14C at 2,178m). Afternoon thunderstorms possible.
race_challenges: Colle delle Finestre: 18.5 km at 9.2% with 7.8 km of unpaved gravel summit at up to 14%
3,170m of total climbing in 103 km — high climbing density
Altitude effects above 2,000m on the Finestre's steepest gradients
Return climb to Sestriere (2,035m) on emptied legs after the Finestre

non_negotiables.requirement: Sustained climbing endurance — 2-3 hour continuous climbing efforts in training
non_negotiables.why: The Colle delle Finestre alone is 18.5 km of relentless 9.2% climbing. You need the muscular endurance to sustain threshold-zone effort for 60-90 minutes on a single climb, then do it again on the return to Sestriere.

non_negotiables.requirement: Road bike setup tested on rough surfaces — tire choice and pressure dialed
non_negotiables.why: The 7.8 km of unpaved Finestre road is rideable on a road bike but demands 25mm+ tires at lower pressure than normal. Test your setup on dirt roads or rough tarmac before race day. A flat tire on a 14% gravel gradient at 2,000m altitude is a race-ending scenario.

non_negotiables.requirement: Full dress rehearsal — 3+ hours of climbing with nutrition and descending strategy
non_negotiables.why: The event demands sustained climbing, a transition to unpaved surface at altitude, and then additional climbing on tired legs. Your nutrition plan, pacing strategy, and gear (arm warmers for the Finestre descent) must be tested in a single long effort.
